items.q: What tool is used to pound nails into wood?
items.explanation: Most hammers have a flat striking face, and many claw hammers also have a split end for pulling nails.

items.q: Iceland lies in the North Atlantic Ocean.
items.explanation: The island sits between Greenland, Norway, and the British Isles near the meeting of North Atlantic and Arctic waters.

items.q: Which continent is home to the Nile River?
items.explanation: The Nile flows north through northeastern Africa and empties into the Mediterranean Sea.

items.q: What material are most drinking glasses and many windows made from?
items.explanation: Common glass is produced by heating silica-rich materials until they melt and then cool into a rigid transparent solid.

items.q: What sign of the zodiac is represented by a crab?
items.explanation: Cancer is one of the twelve zodiac signs and is traditionally associated with dates from late June to late July.

items.q: What vehicle has two wheels and is powered by pedaling?
items.explanation: Modern bicycles became widespread in the late nineteenth century and remain a common form of personal transport.

items.q: The national flag of Japan features a red circle.
items.explanation: The flag, called the Nisshoki or Hinomaru, represents the sun with a centered crimson disc on a white field.

items.q: What kind of home is traditionally made from blocks of packed snow?
items.explanation: Igloos are associated with Arctic peoples and can provide effective insulation against extreme cold.

items.q: What insect is often recognized by its red shell with black spots?
items.explanation: Ladybugs are beetles, and many species are useful in gardens because they eat plant pests such as aphids.

items.q: What tree is known for producing acorns?
items.explanation: Oak trees support many ecosystems, and their acorns are an important food source for wildlife.

items.q: Which cloud type is most strongly associated with thunderstorms?
items.explanation: Cumulonimbus clouds grow vertically through a deep layer of the atmosphere and can produce thunder, lightning, hail, and heavy rain.

items.q: The Odyssey is traditionally attributed to Homer.
items.explanation: Ancient Greek tradition pairs Homer with both the Iliad and the Odyssey, though modern scholarship debates authorship details.

items.q: What is the general term for a grouped set of lines in a poem, often separated by a blank line?
items.explanation: Poets use stanzas to organize rhythm and ideas, much as paragraphs organize prose.

items.q: Which Roman emperor wrote the work now known as Meditations?
items.explanation: Meditations was written as a series of personal reflections and is one of the best-known surviving texts of Stoic philosophy.

items.q: What visual signaling system uses standard flag positions to represent letters of the alphabet?
items.explanation: Semaphore lets a sender encode letters by holding two flags in specific positions, making it useful where distant visual communication is possible.

items.q: What is the point on Earth's surface directly above an earthquake's focus called?
items.explanation: The focus, or hypocenter, is underground where rupture begins, while the epicenter marks the position directly above it at the surface.

items.q: The Caspian Sea is landlocked.
items.explanation: Despite its name, the Caspian has no natural outlet to the ocean and is bordered by five countries.

items.q: Which Egyptian god of embalming and the afterlife is commonly shown with a jackal head?
items.explanation: Anubis was closely linked to mummification rites and the protection of the dead in ancient Egyptian belief.

items.q: What is the name of the tower attached to a mosque from which the call to prayer is traditionally given?
items.explanation: Minarets became a recognizable feature of mosque architecture and served both practical and symbolic purposes in Islamic cities.

items.q: What branch of astronomy studies the origin and evolution of the universe as a whole?
items.explanation: Cosmology examines questions such as the Big Bang, cosmic expansion, and the large-scale structure of the universe.

items.q: In fencing, what convention determines which fencer has priority to score in foil and sabre?
items.explanation: The convention helps judges decide which touch counts when both fencers land nearly simultaneously.

items.q: A sestina traditionally ends with a three-line envoi after six six-line stanzas.
items.explanation: The form is defined by an intricate end-word repetition pattern rather than by rhyme.

items.q: Which Chinese dynasty ruled during Zheng He's treasure voyages?
items.explanation: Zheng He sailed in the early fifteenth century under emperors of the Ming dynasty.

items.q: Which Persian poet wrote the Shahnameh?
items.explanation: The Shahnameh, or Book of Kings, is one of the longest epic poems ever written by a single author.

items.q: In chemistry, what name is given to compounds that share a molecular formula but differ in structure?
items.explanation: Structural differences can give isomers different physical properties and very different biological effects.

items.q: What astronomical term describes the point on the celestial sphere directly above an observer?
items.explanation: The opposite point beneath the observer is the nadir, and both are defined relative to local position on Earth.

items.q: The Hanseatic League was primarily a network of trading cities in northern Europe.
items.explanation: Its merchants dominated Baltic and North Sea trade for centuries through commercial privileges and mutual protection.

items.q: Which painter created The Garden of Earthly Delights?
items.explanation: Bosch's triptych is famous for its dense symbolic imagery and is now housed in Madrid's Prado Museum.

items.q: Which mathematician wrote Arithmetica, a work later linked to Fermat's Last Theorem?
items.explanation: Fermat wrote his famous marginal note beside a problem in a Latin edition of Arithmetica.

items.q: Which dynasty commissioned the Terracotta Army for the tomb of China's first emperor?
items.explanation: The Terracotta Army was buried near the mausoleum of Qin Shi Huang in the third century BCE.
